historical_significance: Oldest largest and most beautiful building in Mandu in Dhar district, Madhya Pradesh. Construction started during time of Hoshang Shah and finished by Mahmud Khilji in 1454. Located in center of Mandu Bazar opposite Ashrafi Mahal. Doorway has inscription indicating mosque modelled on mosque of Damascus. Built in Mughal style of architecture on grand scale. Eastern side main entrance with domed entrance hall and wide flight of steps. Main doorway has marble jambs and lintels typical of Hindu structures. Entry via eastern entrance leads to large courtyard with colonnaded verandas on three sides. Pillared verandas lead to prayer halls covered with 58 small domes and three large domes. Central mihrab decorated with verses from Quran. Raised pulpit at center has miniature pulpit made of marble. Showcases remarkable fusion of Hindu and Islamic architectural styles.

primary_figure_or_concept: Tomb of Sultan Hoshang Shah Ghori
historical_significance: Mausoleum of Sultan Hoshang Shah Ghori, first formally appointed Sultan of Malwa Sultanate of Central India. Part of Village Group or Central Group of Monuments in Mandu. Stands in courtyard to west of Jami Masjid. Construction started by Hoshang Shah and completed by Muhamad Khilji in about 1440 CE. Constructed entirely with white marble, considered first marble mausoleum in India. Precedes Taj Mahal by around 200 years. Historical accounts indicate Emperor Shah Jahan sent team of architects and senior craftsmen to Mandu to study its design and construction. Built in Afghan style of architecture. Square structure on plinth crowned by central dome with smaller domes at corners and overhanging cornice. Interior walls ornamented with exquisite paintings and moldings in form of miniature arches with blue enamel background running through dome's rim. Main sarcophagus shaped like casket with receding bands and mihrab at top with Hindu designs. Small crescent at crown transported from Mesopotamia or Persia.

Ancient structure located in center of Mandu opposite Jama Masjid. Part of Village Group or Central Group of Monuments in Mandu. Initially constructed as Madrasa, Islamic School of learning, by Mahmud Khilji, successor of Hoshang Shah. Contains rows of cells and long corridors cornered with four tall towers entirely looking like school building. Tower of Victory raised by Mahmud Khilji in 1443 to commemorate victory over Rana Khumba of Mewar. Tower built in red sandstone rising through 7 stages of which only one storey survived. Spiral stairway of 198 steps in victory pillar. In local tradition, got name Ashrafi (gold coin) Mahal (palace) associated with Ghiyas-ud-din Khilji who is said to have had 15000 queens. According to legend, when weight of some queens increased, encouraged them to reduce weight by climbing staircase up and down regularly, giving Ashrafi (gold coins) based on number of steps climbed daily.

Stunning 15th-century architectural marvel in ancient city of Mandu. Built during reign of Sultan Ghiyas-ud-din Khilji who in historical tradition is said to have had as many as 15000 women as consorts. To accommodate women belonging to royal consortium, Jahaz Mahal built in second half of 15th century. Situated between two picturesque lakes, Munj Talao and Kapur Talao. Palace derives name from unique ship-like structure and reflection on surrounding water creating illusion of floating ship. One of buildings making up royal palace complex at Mandu which consists of Jahaz Mahal, Hindola Mahal, Taveli Mahal and Nahar Jharokha. Elegant example of Mandu architecture.

Large meeting hall or durbar in ancient city of Mandu. One of buildings making up royal palace complex at Mandu. Might have been constructed in 1425 during reign of Hoshang Shah but may date to end of 15th century during reign of Ghiyas-ud-din. Palace majorly used by Mughals for leisure. Today tourist destination in ruined city. Important example of Mandu architecture.

Sunni Friday mosque in Burhanpur, Madhya Pradesh. Monument of National Importance. One of major mosques built during Faroqui rule in city. Initial Jama Masjid built by Azam Humayun 1421 CE to north of city at Etwara called Bibi Ki Masjid. Present Jama Masjid built by ruler Adil Shah in center of town. Construction started 1590 completed 5 years later. Built in Islamic architecture. Roofless mosque but intersecting symmetrical arches create ceiling. Facade has 15 arches flanked by two minarets 36m in height rising majestically across Burhanpur skyline. Built using black stone brought from Mandu hence also called Kali Masjid. 17 Mihrab niches facing direction of Mecca. One Mihrab holds Arabic inscription mentioning patron along with verses of Quran citing year of construction. Small pond within premises. Only mosque in India with bilingual inscriptions in Sanskrit and Arabic at south end of prayer hall mentioning astronomical position date and year according to Hindu Samvat calendar.

Mosque in Bhopal commissioned by Sikandar Jahan completed 1860. Built by Sikander Jehan Begum of Bhopal. Significant historical landmark in Bhopal. 19th-century white marble mosque famed for striking red towers and serene ambiance. Mosque's architecture inspired by famous Jama Masjid in Delhi featuring beautiful white marble facade with two small cupolas on top. Two dark red towers on either side of primary structure. Exquisite blend of red-brick facade and white marble exterior. Two majestic dark red minarets adorned with golden spikes. Intricate stone carvings resembling delicate flowers. Main hall crafted from pristine white marble with decorated archway. Two ablution tanks near eastern gate with marble fountains. Entire mosque built on raised platform.

Mosque in Bhopal. Largest mosque in India by area. Commissioned by Nawab Begum Shahjahan around 1871-1887 built along with surrounding neighbourhood of Shahjahanabad. Construction remained incomplete for long period due to lack of funds. In 1971 construction resumed by Moulana Mohammad Imran Khan of Bhopal with contributions from Muslim community. Mosque finally completed in 1985. Can accommodate approximately 12000 worshippers in built structure. Courtyard reported to accommodate up to 175000 people during prayer. Architectural wonder built by woman. Sprawling construction in heart of city. Meaning of name 'Crown of Mosques'. Impressive structure made in red sandstone. Two gigantic white minarets with domes on top. Entire mosque placed on pink facade. Floor of main hallway made of marble decorated with trellised screens with fine artwork. 11 arches make way to Quibla wall noted for huge pillars touching ceilings designed in petal motifs.

Revered Muslim shrine located on outskirts of Jaora town in Ratlam district, Madhya Pradesh. Dedicated to shrine of Hussain Tekri. In devotional tradition, believed to be spiritually significant site. Visited by devotees who come to seek blessings and offer prayers. Built in 19th century by Mohammad Iftikhar Ali Khan Bahadur, Nawab of Jaora. Located on hill called Tekri offering panoramic views of surrounding area. Adorned with intricate carvings and beautiful white marble facade. Home to 6 shrines. Dargah has mosque and madrasa. Mosque beautiful structure with domed roof and minarets accommodating several hundred worshippers. Revered by Shias, Sunnis and Hindus.

Historic mosque located in Raisen Fort, Madhya Pradesh. Part of fort complex. Important mosque from medieval period. Raisen Fort significant historical site in central India. Mosque represents Islamic heritage of region.

Major mosque in Khajrana area of Indore, Madhya Pradesh. Important religious center for Muslim community of Indore. Active Friday mosque serving large congregation. Well-maintained mosque with traditional architecture.
